name: System Design — APIs, Traffic, Async, Observability
description: REST/gRPC, HTTP semantics, rate-limiting algorithms, queues vs streams, delivery guarantees, observability pillars.

cards.definition: Use plural nouns for collections (/users, /orders), nest by ownership (/users/42/orders), avoid verbs in paths (use HTTP methods to express the action). Filter via query params (?status=active), not segments.
GET — read, idempotent, cacheable, no body. POST — create, NOT idempotent. PUT — replace whole resource, idempotent. PATCH — partial update, NOT necessarily idempotent. DELETE — remove, idempotent.
200 OK — generic success with body. 201 Created — new resource (return its location). 202 Accepted — async work queued. 204 No Content — success with empty body (DELETE, PUT without representation).

400 Bad Request (malformed input). 401 Unauthorized (no/invalid auth). 403 Forbidden (auth ok, denied). 404 Not Found. 409 Conflict (concurrent edit). 422 Unprocessable Entity (validation). 429 Too Many Requests (rate limit).

500 Internal Server Error (unhandled). 502 Bad Gateway (upstream failed). 503 Service Unavailable (overloaded; Retry-After). 504 Gateway Timeout (upstream slow). 503 and 504 are clearly transient/retryable; 500 may not be (could be a deterministic bug). Most 4xx are permanent (don't retry).
gRPC: HTTP/2 + Protobuf, schema-first via .proto, binary wire, streaming, low overhead. REST: HTTP/1.1+JSON, schema-loose, browser-native, debuggable. Internal RPC: gRPC. Public/browser APIs: REST.

Unary — one req, one resp. Server streaming — one req, stream of resps (e.g. progress updates). Client streaming — stream of reqs, one resp (e.g. file upload). Bidirectional — both stream concurrently (e.g. chat).
Encode the position (e.g. last id + sort key) in an opaque cursor returned with each page. Stable across inserts, no offset cost. Tradeoff: can't skip arbitrary pages. Default for high-scale APIs.
?page=3&size=20. Easy to implement but expensive at scale (DB scans page*size rows) and unstable (inserts shift items). Fine for small/static datasets.

URL path: /v1/users (visible, breaks proxies less). Header: Accept: application/vnd.api.v2+json (cleaner URLs, harder to debug). Pick one and apply consistently. Deprecate via Sunset header + Warning headers.

Client passes a unique key with non-idempotent requests (POST /payments). Server stores (key, response) and replays the response on retry. Standard for payment APIs (Stripe, etc.). Server uses a unique index on the key column to prevent races on concurrent retries.
A bucket of capacity B refills at rate R tokens/sec. Each request consumes 1 token; if empty, reject (or queue). Allows bursts up to B; sustained rate ≤ R. Used by AWS API Gateway, Stripe.
Requests enter a fixed-size queue draining at rate R. Smooths traffic to a constant output. Bursts queue up; overflow drops. Best when downstream needs steady load (no bursts).
Count requests per fixed time window (e.g. per minute). Reset at boundaries. Cheap (single counter). Flaw: 2× burst at boundary (max in last sec of window N + first sec of window N+1).
Two adjacent fixed windows; current rate = current_count + previous_count × (overlap fraction). Smooths the boundary burst from fixed window. Cheap (two counters). Standard production choice.
Store a timestamp per request; count those within the last N seconds. Most accurate. Memory cost grows with QPS — don't use at scale without a sampling/eviction strategy.

Rate limiting: hard cap, reject when exceeded (return 429). Throttling: slow down to a steady pace (queue/delay), often via leaky bucket. Throttling preserves availability under burst; rate limiting enforces fairness/quotas.

Downstream signals upstream to slow down when overloaded — instead of accepting more work and dying. Implemented via bounded queues (block/reject on full), TCP windows, or explicit pause/resume signals.
Reject lower-priority requests when at capacity to keep critical paths healthy. Better than letting everything degrade. Tier requests by importance; shed bottom tiers first.

Queue: each message goes to exactly one consumer; deleted after ack. Use for task distribution. Pub/sub: each message goes to all subscribers; retained per consumer. Use for event broadcasting. Kafka does both via consumer groups.

Both. A topic is a distributed log; multiple consumer groups can independently read it (pub/sub). Within a group, each partition is assigned to one consumer (queue). This + durability + throughput is why Kafka is the default at scale.

One event → many consumers (user_signed_up → email + analytics + CRM + onboarding). Hard part: every consumer must process reliably; one slow consumer can't block the rest. Typical impl: SNS→SQS, Kafka multi-consumer-group.

Many sources → one sink (sensors → telemetry aggregator). Hard part: ordering across producers, deduplication, varying arrival rates. Typical impl: single Kafka topic + partition key + buffered aggregator.
Fire-and-forget; messages can be lost but never duplicated. No retries. Use for low-value telemetry where loss is acceptable.
Producer retries until ack'd; consumers must be idempotent because duplicates happen. The realistic default for production messaging.

Idempotent producer + transactional commit + coordinated consumer. Kafka supports it via transactions but adds latency and complexity. Most systems achieve effectively-exactly-once via at-least-once + idempotent consumer instead.

Messages that fail processing repeatedly (e.g. poison pill, bad data) are routed here after N retries instead of blocking the main queue. Operators triage manually. Without a DLQ, one bad message can stall everything behind it.

Write the business change AND an event row to the same DB transaction (an 'outbox' table). A separate publisher reads the outbox and publishes to the broker. Solves dual-write atomicity (DB + queue) without 2PC.
Persist every state change as an event; current state is derived by replaying events. Full audit log, time travel, easy event-driven downstream. Tradeoff: replay cost, schema evolution is harder.

Metrics — numeric, aggregated, cheap, alertable; tells you THAT something is wrong. Logs — discrete events, full context; tells you WHY for a specific event. Traces — per-request paths through services; tells you WHERE time was spent.

A trace ID generated at the entry point propagates through every downstream call (HTTP header / gRPC metadata). Each service emits spans (start/duration/parent span id). Spans assemble into a flame graph in Jaeger/Zipkin/Datadog APM.

Head: decide at trace start (e.g. 1% of requests). Cheap, predictable. Tail: buffer trace, decide after — keep all errors and slow requests. More useful, more expensive. Production usually combines: 1% head + 100% of errors via tail.

'Is this process alive?' Failure → kill + restart container. Used to catch deadlocks/hangs. MUST be lightweight — don't check downstream deps, or every pod restarts when the DB blips. Pairs with readiness, never duplicates it.

'Is this instance ready to serve traffic?' Failure → drain from load balancer (no restart). Used during startup warmup or transient dependency outage. Can check critical deps. Failure should not co-restart the pod.
SLI — the metric (request success rate, p99 latency). SLO — internal target (99.9% of requests <500ms). SLA — contractual external commitment with penalties; usually looser than SLO to keep a buffer.

1 − SLO. 99.9% SLO = 0.1% allowed unavailability ≈ 8.7 hours/year. When the budget is full, slow releases and focus on reliability. When healthy, ship riskier changes. Reframes 'reliability vs velocity' as 'how much budget to spend?'.
